3.note: The burnt offering must be 'without blemish' (tāmîm) — the unblemished animal is the sine qua non of acceptable sacrifice. Hebrews 9:14 applies this requirement directly to Christ: 'how much more will the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God, purify our conscience.' The tāmîm requirement was always measuring toward a sacrifice the law itself could not supply.

Peter uses the same Levitical standard when describing redemption's cost: 'the precious blood of Christ, like that of a lamb without blemish or spot.' The burnt offering's unblemished lamb is the explicit type Peter cites to explain why Christ's blood achieves what the yearly temple sacrifices only rehearsed.

Paul calls believers to 'present your bodies as a living sacrifice, holy and acceptable to God' — the language of Leviticus 1:3's 'willing offering accepted before the LORD.' The burnt offering, given wholly to God and consumed entirely, becomes the image of total self-consecration that the gospel demands of those justified in Christ.

4.note: The offerer lays his hand on the head of the burnt offering, identifying himself with it so that 'it shall be accepted for him to make atonement for him.' This hand-laying transfers guilt: the animal bears what the worshiper deserves. Hebrews 9:28 declares the antitype: 'Christ, having been offered once to bear the sins of many.' The gesture enacted what the cross accomplished.

He shall lay his hand on the head of the burnt offering and it shall be accepted for him to make atonement — the laying of hands transfers the offerer's identity to the animal; this identification mechanism is the type of Christ's substitution: he bore our sins in his body on the tree (1 Pet 2:24)

9.note: The burnt offering washed and burned is 'a food offering with a pleasing aroma to the LORD' (rêaḥ nîḥōaḥ; LXX osmē euōdias). Paul quotes this exact phrase when he describes Christ's sacrifice: 'Christ loved us and gave himself up for us, a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God' (osmē euōdias). The incense-ascent of the burnt offering is the OT frame for the NT atonement.

13.note: Every grain offering must be salted — 'you shall not let the salt of the covenant of your God be missing.' Salt preserves from corruption and seals covenants. Jesus' saying 'everyone will be salted with fire' (Mark 9:49) and 'have salt in yourselves' (9:50) draws on Levitical salt-covenant language: the disciple's life, like the grain offering, must carry the integrity of covenant faithfulness.

'Season all your grain offerings with salt' — the covenant-preserving quality of salt transfers in Paul's instruction: 'let your speech always be gracious, seasoned with salt' (Col 4:6). Speech that bears witness to the gospel carries the same covenant-integrity function that salt bore in the grain offering: preserving, purifying, marking covenant identity.

1.note: The peace offering (šělāmîm) is the sacrifice of reconciliation — offered when enmity has been resolved and relationship restored. Paul declares the antitype: 'he himself is our peace, who has made us both one and has broken down in his flesh the dividing wall of hostility.' What the šělāmîm enacted through animal blood, Christ achieved through his own flesh.

The peace offering establishes šālôm between God and worshiper — fellowship through the blood. Colossians 1:20: 'through him to reconcile to himself all things, whether on earth or in heaven, making peace by the blood of his cross.' The cosmic scope of Christ's reconciliation corresponds to and vastly exceeds the individual peace offerings of the Mosaic system.

The peace offering's existence presupposes prior enmity now resolved: the worshiper brings it in a state of restored relationship. Paul's declaration 'since we have been justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ' (Rom 5:1) is the verbal announcement of what the šělāmîm enacted ritually — access to God through a mediating sacrifice.

17.note: The absolute prohibition on eating blood ('a statute forever throughout your generations') underlies the Jerusalem Council's decree that Gentile believers should 'abstain from blood' (Acts 15:20, 29). The council preserves this element of the Mosaic code not as a salvation requirement but as a bridge for Jewish-Gentile fellowship — acknowledging the deep theological weight of blood as the seat of life (Lev 17:11).

3.note: The sin offering regulation for the anointed priest specifies he must offer for his own sin as well as the people's — because 'he too is subject to weakness' (Heb 5:2-3). The author of Hebrews uses this to distinguish Christ: unlike every Aaronic high priest who had to offer for himself, 'he has no need, like those high priests, to offer sacrifices daily...since he did this once for all when he offered up himself' (7:27).

12.note: The sin-offering bull must be carried 'outside the camp' and burned — it is not eaten but destroyed beyond the sanctuary perimeter. The author of Hebrews identifies this as the direct type of the crucifixion: 'the bodies of those animals whose blood is brought into the holy places by the high priest as a sacrifice for sin are burned outside the camp. So Jesus also suffered outside the gate in order to sanctify the people through his own blood' (Heb 13:11-12).

20.note: 'The priest shall make atonement for them, and they shall be forgiven' — the repeated refrain of Leviticus 4 (vv.20, 26, 31, 35) establishes the blood-forgiveness axiom. Hebrews 9:22 draws the principle: 'without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness of sins.' The Levitical refrain is the empirical basis for what Hebrews states as the theological principle.

The refrain 'they shall be forgiven' after each sin offering creates an irony Hebrews exploits: 'it is impossible for the blood of bulls and goats to take away sins' (10:4). The forgiveness the sin offerings granted was real — based on the promise the offerings pointed to — but the blood was insufficient in itself. The annual repetition was itself the confession of inadequacy.

32.note: The sin offering for ordinary people is 'a female lamb without blemish.' The specification of a lamb for the sin offering (alongside the later Passover lamb and the Day of Atonement goat) converges on John the Baptist's announcement: 'Behold the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world!' The Levitical lamb that covered individual, class-specific sin is replaced by the Lamb whose offering covers the world's sin.

5.note: When anyone is guilty... they must confess the sin they have committed — the Levitical guilt-offering sequence begins with confession (Hebrew: hitwadhah — to acknowledge, confess). John's foundational promise presupposes the same logic: if we confess our sins, God is faithful and just to forgive. The new covenant forgiveness operates on the same confession-and-cleansing structure that the guilt-offering system established, with Christ's blood as the offering that makes confession effective.

6.note: He brings as his guilt offering a female animal — the priest makes atonement for his sin and he is forgiven — Hebrews 9:22 articulates the principle underlying the entire offering system: 'without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness.' Every guilt offering in Leviticus 5 is an instance of this axiom; the entire system points to the one definitive blood-shedding that makes forgiveness permanent rather than repeated.

7.note: If he cannot afford a lamb, he brings two turtledoves or two young pigeons — Luke records that Mary and Joseph brought 'a pair of turtledoves or two young pigeons' at Jesus's presentation (Lk 2:24), citing Leviticus 12:8. This is the graduated offering for the poor prescribed in Leviticus 5:7 and mirrored in 12:8; the family's use of this offering signals their economic position: they could not afford a lamb. The incarnation's poverty is encoded in the offering type.

15.note: If anyone violates the sacred things of the LORD unintentionally — a guilt offering is required — Hebrews 9:7 notes that the high priest entered the Holy of Holies only once a year, and then with blood 'which he offered for himself and for the sins committed in ignorance by the people.' The involuntary-sin category that structures Leviticus 5:15-19 is the theological background for Hebrews' explanation of why the old system required repetition. Christ's offering covers both deliberate and unintentional transgressions permanently.

Even without knowing it, one bears their iniquity — the Levitical recognition that ignorance does not exempt from guilt is the covenantal basis for Paul's universal verdict: 'all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God.' The Levitical system acknowledged that sin exists and damages the divine-human relationship even when the transgressor was unaware, which is exactly Paul's point about the scope of the human problem.

12.note: The fire on the altar must be kept burning; it must not go out — the perpetual altar fire, requiring daily priestly attention, images the unceasing nature of atonement-provision. Hebrews 7:25 says Christ 'always lives to intercede' for those who come to God through him — the once-for-all offering that never goes out, the perpetual intercession that replaces the daily priestly fire-keeping. The unquenchable altar is fulfilled in the permanently effective high-priestly ministry of the Son.

27.note: Whatever touches the flesh of the sin offering becomes holy — the holiness-transfer property of the sin offering blood (touching it sanctifies; contact with a garment required washing the garment in the sanctuary) is the Levitical background for Hebrews' argument: if the blood of goats and bulls and the ashes of a heifer sanctify those who are defiled, how much more will the blood of Christ purify the conscience. The Levitical holiness-transfer principle is transposed from ritual to moral purification.

Whatever touches its flesh becomes holy — Paul's argument that the unbelieving spouse is sanctified through the believing one (1 Cor 7:14) may draw on this Levitical holiness-transfer principle. The contact-holiness logic — that proximity to what is holy communicates holiness — runs through the Levitical system and resurfaces in Paul's discussion of mixed households.

30.note: No sin offering whose blood is brought into the tent of meeting to make atonement in the holy place may be eaten — it must be burned with fire — Hebrews 13:11-12 explicitly develops this as a type: 'the bodies of those animals whose blood is brought into the holy place by the high priest as a sin offering are burned outside the camp; therefore Jesus also suffered outside the gate, so that he might sanctify the people through his own blood.' The instruction to burn the sin offering whose blood entered the sanctuary outside the camp is the OT type for the crucifixion outside Jerusalem's walls.

11.note: This is the law of the fellowship offering — the fellowship sacrifice included thanksgiving (todah), vow, and freewill components. Hebrews 13:15 explicitly names the NT fulfillment: 'through him then let us continually offer up a sacrifice of praise to God, that is, the fruit of lips that acknowledge his name.' The Levitical thanksgiving sacrifice (todah) is the OT type for the NT sacrifice of praise — verbal acknowledgment of God's saving acts replacing the animal substitute. The ancient Jewish tradition also connected the todah specifically to the anticipated world-to-come where all other sacrifices would cease but the thanksgiving offering would remain.

12.note: If he offers it as a thanksgiving sacrifice — the fellowship thanksgiving offering (todah) is the specific sacrifice Psalm 116:17 names as the response to divine deliverance from death: 'I will offer you the sacrifice of thanksgiving and call on the name of the LORD.' The todah formula — testimony, praise, shared meal — becomes the model for the NT thanksgiving that the redeemed community offers through Christ.

20.note: A person who eats the fellowship offering in a state of ritual uncleanness shall be cut off from the people — the severe consequence of eating the holy meal in an unworthy state is the background for Paul's solemn warning about the Lord's Supper: 'whoever eats the bread or drinks the cup of the Lord in an unworthy manner will be guilty of the body and blood of the Lord.' The new covenant fellowship meal carries the same holiness-requirement that the Levitical fellowship offering assumed; eating it without discernment repeats the Levitical offense.

26.note: You shall eat no blood of any kind — the prohibition on blood consumption is one of the few Levitical laws the Jerusalem Council declared binding on Gentile believers in Acts 15:29: 'abstain from... blood.' The Council draws on the Noahic (Gen 9:4) and Levitical (Lev 7:26-27; 17:10-14) prohibitions to set a floor for the covenant community's table fellowship, making the blood-prohibition a bridge between the Levitical system and the new covenant community composed of both Jews and Gentiles.

37.note: This is the law of the burnt offering, the grain offering, the sin offering, the guilt offering... — the comprehensive summary of the sacrificial system at the close of Leviticus 7 underscores exactly what Hebrews 10:1 calls 'a shadow of the good things to come and not the very form of those things.' The entire catalogue of offerings listed here is the shadow-system whose repeated enactment demonstrated that it could never achieve perfect cleansing — and whose repeated sacrifice was itself the testimony that the definitive offering was still awaited.

2.note: But into the second tent only the high priest goes, and only once a year, and not without blood — the structure of Yom Kippur (Lev 16) is Hebrews' central OT text for demonstrating that the Levitical system was designed to be temporary and pointing forward to a greater reality: Christ enters the true holy of holies once for all by his own blood

15.note: He shall make atonement for the Holy Place because of the uncleannesses of the people — Aaron enters with the blood of a bull and a goat (Lev 16:14-15); Christ by his own blood entered once for all into the holy places (Heb 9:12), securing eternal redemption rather than annual covering

The blood of bulls and goats cannot take away sins — the Yom Kippur ritual (Lev 16) required annual repetition because animal blood could not permanently remove sin-guilt; this annual repetition was the system's built-in indication of its provisional character

20.note: The live goat — the scapegoat (Azazel) that bears the people's sins and is sent into the wilderness; Behold the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world: Christ is both the sacrificed goat whose blood atones (v. 15) and the scapegoat who removes sin by bearing it away

29.note: The bodies of those animals whose blood is brought into the holy places are burned outside the camp — Jesus also suffered outside the gate in order to sanctify the people through his own blood (Heb 13:12); the burning of the sin-offering carcass outside the camp is the type of the crucifixion outside Jerusalem

11.note: For the life of the flesh is in the blood and I have given it for you on the altar to make atonement for your souls — the blood-for-life principle (Lev 17:11) is the theological foundation of all sacrificial atonement; without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness of sins (Heb 9:22) applies this principle to Christ's death

18.note: You shall love your neighbor as yourself — the great commandment from the Holiness Code (Lev 19:18); Jesus names this as the second great commandment and Paul calls it the fulfillment of the whole law (Gal 5:14; Rom 13:9); James calls it the royal law (Jas 2:8)

For the whole law is fulfilled in one word: You shall love your neighbor as yourself — Paul cites Lev 19:18 as the summary of the entire Mosaic law's ethical demand; the law's social obligations are summed in neighbor-love, which is the character of the Spirit-filled life

12.note: I will walk among you and will be your God and you shall be my people — the covenant formula of Lev 26:12 is the OT's recurring covenant-relationship phrase (also Ezek 36:28; Jer 31:33); Paul quotes it in 2 Cor 6:16 as the basis for the church as the temple of the living God; it is fulfilled in the Spirit's indwelling of the new covenant community
